title: Bulls Engage Search Firm To Fill Basketball Operations Vacancy
summary: The Chicago Bulls plan to hire a search firm to identify a new head of basketball operations, sources told ESPN. The team dismissed vice president Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley after six seasons leading the organization. Team president and CEO Michael Reinsdorf signaled a deliberate change in approach from the 2020 process that landed Karnisovas. "I think this process is going to be very different," Reinsdorf said. Karnisovas and Eversley compiled a 224-254 record over six years, producing one winning season and one playoff appearance. This marks only the second leadership change in basketball operations since 2003, when John Paxson held the role for 17 consecutive years before Karnisovas took over as he also shared duties with Gar Forman for parts of those years. Despite the team's poor record, the opening is considered an appealing opportunity. The Bulls hold their own lottery pick, roughly $60 million in projected cap space and several young players on team-friendly contracts. Josh Giddey and Matas Buzelis are Chicago's two foundational young players. Billy Donovan is expected to be retained in some capacity following the season's conclusion.

title: Michael Malone Signs Six-Year, $50M Deal With North Carolina
summary: North Carolina has officially hired former Denver Nuggets head coach Michael Malone, agreeing to a six-year contract worth approximately $50 million while averaging more than $8.3 million per year, sources confirmed to ESPN. The figure places Malone among the five highest-paid coaches in college basketball. Steve Kerr is currently the NBA's highest paid head coach at $17.5 million per season , but an $8.3 million salary would put Malone in the top-10 of NBA coaches ahead of JJ Redick at $8 million. "Carolina is one of the most historic programs in college basketball, and I am honored to be the head coach of the Tar Heels," Malone said in a statement. "I will do everything I can to continue UNC's championship legacy while preparing our players for professional careers and life after basketball." Malone, 52, compiled a 471-327 record across 10 seasons in Denver and led the Nuggets to the franchise's only NBA title in 2023. He was dismissed last April and joined ESPN as an analyst the following month. He has not coached at the college level since serving as a Manhattan assistant in 2001. Malone was recommended by a search committee led by executive associate athletic director Steve Newmark and director of athletics Bubba Cunningham. Malone had new ties to the Tar Heels as his daughter is on the volleyball team. UNC had pursued several prominent college coaches, including Michigan's Dusty May, Arizona's Tommy Lloyd and Iowa State's T.J. Otzelberger, before all three publicly committed to remaining at their current programs. "[Malone] is a brilliant coach who will deliver a modern and disciplined approach to leading our men's basketball program," executive associate athletic director Steve Newmark said. Malone inherits a program in transition. Former coach Hubert Davis was dismissed March 24, five days after the Tar Heels surrendered a 19-point lead in a first-round NCAA tournament loss to 11th-seeded VCU.

title: Louisville's Mikel Brown Jr. Declares For 2026 NBA Draft, Projected Top-10 Pick
summary: Louisville freshman guard Mikel Brown Jr. has announced his intention to enter the 2026 NBA Draft, ESPN Andscape reported. ESPN's Jeremy Woo currently projects the 6-foot-5 guard as the seventh overall selection. Brown averaged 18.2 points, 4.7 assists and 3.3 rebounds across 21 games this season before a lower back injury ended his year. Brown had a PER of 20.8 . He missed 14 games, including both the ACC and NCAA tournaments. "Obviously, it's just been a dream of mine to be able to be in this position," Brown said. "The little kid in me would freaking scream just to know that I have an opportunity to play at the highest level, which has been a dream of mine since I started picking up a basketball." Brown delivered one of Louisville's most memorable individual performances on February 9, scoring 45 points against NC State. The effort tied Wes Unseld's 1967 program record and set a new ACC freshman scoring mark. Still, Brown considers his 29-point effort in a November victory over rival Kentucky his true breakout moment. "That Kentucky game is when I felt like I just made a statement like, 'Hey, I'm here. I'm here to stay,'" Brown said. Health remains the central question surrounding his draft stock. Brown said his back is improving with daily treatment and he expects to participate in predraft workouts and NBA Summer League in July. One NBA scout said Brown remains a projected top-10 pick despite the injury concern. Brown consulted with Cleveland Cavaliers guard Donovan Mitchell, a Louisville alumnus and seven-time All-Star, before declaring. Mitchell has served as a mentor since the two connected during Brown's junior year of high school. Brown will be represented by Seros Partners.

title: Nando De Colo Announces Plans To Retire
summary: Veteran French guard Nando De Colo announced that he plans to retire at the end of this season. De Colo is wrapping up his season and career by playing for Fenerbahce in Turkey. De Colo played for two seasons in the NBA for the San Antonio Spurs and Toronto Raptors. He then returned to Europe and spent the next five seasons with CSKA Moscow. De Colo led CSKA to the 2016 EuroLeague title. In 2019, De Colo left for his first run with Fenerbahce. After three seasons with the Turkish powerhouse, the veteran guard spent three years with ASVEL in his native France, before returning to Fenerbahce this season. De Colo has also been a fixture for the French National team. He helped France to win silver medals in 2020 and 2024, gold at the 2013 EuroBasket and bronze at the 2019 FIBA World Cup.

title: Heat Came Closest To Landing Giannis Antetokounmpo At Trade Deadline
summary: The Miami Heat constructed a trade package centered on Tyler Herro, Kel'el Ware and multiple draft picks that brought them closer than any other franchise to acquiring Giannis Antetokounmpo at the February 5 trade deadline. Milwaukee Bucks ownership intervened at the deadline, according to multiple league sources. The Bucks seriously weighed Miami's offer on February 4, one day before the deadline. Ownership ultimately concluded that holding Antetokounmpo and pursuing a stronger return in the summer represented the superior path. The Bucks informed the Heat on the morning of February 5 that no deal would be completed. The Minnesota Timberwolves and Golden State Warriors also engaged in substantive negotiations with Milwaukee. More than a dozen teams contacted the Bucks ahead of the deadline. League sources said executives from Miami, Minnesota and Golden State all believed general manager Jon Horst was negotiating genuinely, seeking a young blue-chip player and a substantial draft-pick haul in return. The Heat's offer aligned with Milwaukee's stated criteria more closely than competing proposals. Herro provides proven offensive production as a scorer and shot-creator, while Kel'el Ware represented the young blue-chip talent the Bucks sought. The accompanying draft compensation added further incentive. Milwaukee's asking price proved prohibitive for other suitors. The Bucks sought Evan Mobley from the Cleveland Cavaliers and VJ Edgecombe from the Philadelphia 76ers, plus additional assets from both franchises simultaneously. Several teams grew frustrated when Bucks officials took days to return calls, leading executives to question whether Milwaukee was ever genuinely committed to completing a deal. Antetokounmpo's contractual situation complicated every negotiation. No team was willing to surrender a franchise-caliber package without assurance he would sign a long-term extension. He becomes eligible for a four-year, $275 million contract either October 1 if he remains in Milwaukee, or six months after any trade is completed. "One of two things will happen: Either he will be extended, or he'll be traded," Bucks co-owner Wes Edens said in March. The Heat now enter the offseason as a likely frontrunner should Milwaukee revisit trade discussions this summer. Bucks ownership has since acknowledged the probability of an Antetokounmpo deal coming to fruition, with the organization's highest-ranking officials aligned on that outcome, sources told ESPN. Whether Miami's revised summer offer will satisfy Milwaukee's elevated asking price remains the central question.

title: Giannis Antetokounmpo's Push For Amicable Trade From Bucks Has Now Become 'Toxic'
summary: Giannis Antetokounmpo spent months attempting to handle his departure from the Milwaukee Bucks with discretion. What began as a professional, behind-the-scenes push for a trade has fractured into one of the most combustible franchise situations in the NBA. "This is as toxic of a team situation as any in the league," one source close to the team told ESPN. "They waited until the very end on Giannis, and now everyone knows." Antetokounmpo raised serious concerns about Milwaukee's roster before the 2025-26 season even tipped off. The Bucks had spent the better part of a decade constructing contending rosters, but the blockbuster acquisitions of Jrue Holiday and Damian Lillard came at a steep cost. Milwaukee surrendered three first-round picks and four pick swaps in those deals, stripping the franchise of the tradeable assets needed to retool after three straight first-round playoff exits. Antetokounmpo privately told the organization for months leading up to the February 5 trade deadline that he was ready to be moved. He stopped short of issuing a formal public trade demand but made his position unambiguous to all parties involved. He believed both sides needed to separate immediately, as the franchise lacked the foundation to compete. "Giannis has wanted to handle this professionally by being very up front with the team," one source with direct knowledge of the situation said. "This could have been a happy resolution but instead might end up being a nasty breakup." The Bucks declined to move him at the deadline despite fielding calls from more than a dozen teams. The Minnesota Timberwolves, Miami Heat and Golden State Warriors were among the leading suitors. Milwaukee came closest to accepting Miami's offer, built around Tyler Herro, Kel'el Ware and multiple draft picks, before ownership pulled back on February 4. The relationship between Antetokounmpo and general manager Jon Horst has deteriorated sharply since the deadline passed, sources said. An NBA investigation into whether Milwaukee engaged in tanking by keeping Antetokounmpo sidelined added another layer of institutional conflict, with the two sides delivering contradictory accounts to league investigators. Milwaukee's highest-ranking officials have now accepted that a trade is the probable outcome this offseason. The Bucks enter the summer holding three first-round picks to offer, along with the question of whether any team will surrender sufficient assets without a firm long-term commitment from Antetokounmpo. "When your best player is one foot in, one foot out," said one team source, "you're not going to win."

title: Giannis Antetokounmpo Lost Nike Bonus Tied To Games Played After Bucks Refused To Clear Him
summary: The Milwaukee Bucks' decision to keep Giannis Antetokounmpo out of the lineup down the stretch cost him a meaningful financial penalty beyond the court, according to multiple sources. Milwaukee's choice to sit their franchise player triggered the loss of a substantial bonus in his Nike endorsement contract, which required him to appear in at least 41 games to collect. Antetokounmpo has played in just 36 games this season, a career low. He suffered five separate injuries and missed extended stretches throughout the year. After hyperextending his left knee on March 15 against the Indiana Pacers, the Bucks asked him to shut down for the remainder of the season. Antetokounmpo refused and he told the team he intended to return once healthy and pushed to play against the Utah Jazz on March 17. The Bucks' medical staff declined to clear him. He subsequently expressed hope of playing later on the road trip but never returned to action. Doc Rivers separately told a group of veterans, including Kyle Kuzma and Myles Turner, that ownership expected no one to sit out due to illegitimate injuries. That directive, however, was never extended to Antetokounmpo, sources told ESPN. The NBA launched a formal investigation after the National Basketball Players Association released a statement on March 24 implying Milwaukee was damaging league integrity by keeping Antetokounmpo sidelined. The league interviewed Antetokounmpo, his representation, Bucks officials and team doctors, an inquiry that Antetokounmpo himself pushed the union and league to conduct. "For somebody to come and tell me to not play or not to compete, it's like a slap in my face," Antetokounmpo told reporters on April 3rd. "I'm available to play. Right now. I'm available. So, I don't know where the relationship goes from there." The Bucks told investigators that Antetokounmpo declined to participate in three-on-three scrimmages as part of his return-to-play protocol, a claim Antetokounmpo disputed.

title: Giannis Antetokounmpo, Agent Met With Bucks In January To Invoke Handshake Trade Agreement
summary: Giannis Antetokounmpo and his agent, Alex Saratsis , met directly with Milwaukee Bucks owners Jimmy Haslam and Wes Edens in late January to discuss a handshake agreement tied to his 2020 and 2023 contract extensions. The two sides had previously agreed that ownership would facilitate a collaborative trade should the time come, according to multiple league sources. Shortly after that meeting, Antetokounmpo met with general manager Jon Horst at the team's practice facility. Horst indicated he was actively pursuing trade negotiations and told Antetokounmpo he might not be on the roster when the 2026-27 season begins, multiple sources confirmed. More than a dozen teams contacted the Milwaukee Bucks ahead of the February 5 trade deadline. The Minnesota Timberwolves, Miami Heat and Golden State Warriors emerged as the primary suitors. League sources said executives from all three franchises believed Horst was negotiating in good faith, seeking a young blue-chip player and/or a substantial package of draft picks in return. The Heat's offer, centered on Tyler Herro, Kel'el Ware and multiple draft picks, drew serious internal consideration. Milwaukee came close to accepting on February 4 before ownership decided to hold Antetokounmpo and pursue a stronger offer in the summer. Some rival executives grew skeptical of Milwaukee's commitment after the Bucks took days to return calls and messages. Others balked at asking prices that included Evan Mobley from the Cleveland Cavaliers and VJ Edgecombe from the Philadelphia 76ers, along with additional assets from both teams. The Wolves and Warriors ultimately sensed Milwaukee would retain Antetokounmpo and shifted focus to completing other deadline transactions. Antetokounmpo holds one guaranteed season remaining on his contract, with a player option in 2027. He becomes eligible for a four-year, $275 million extension on October 1 if he remains with Milwaukee, or six months after a trade with a new team. "One of two things will happen: Either he will be extended, or he'll be traded," Edens told ESPN in March.

McKay will also be supported by Trae Young, who was named assistant GM last spring.

summary: The Washington Mystics parted ways with general manager Jamila Wideman on Monday. "At this time, we are looking forward to this historic new season for the WNBA and remain focused on building sustainable success in Washington. I have every confidence in the team we have, and will continue to build, to move us toward our goals." Coach Sydney Johnson is set to take over control of the basketball operations. title: Michigan Holds Off UConn 69-63 To Win NCAA Title
summary: Michigan captured its first national championship in 36 years Monday night, defeating UConn 69-63 behind a dominant second-half performance. Guard Elliot Cadeau earned Most Outstanding Player honors with 19 points. The Wolverines became the first program to defeat UConn in the Sweet 16 or beyond since Michigan State in the 2009 Final Four. Michigan controlled the paint and free throw line, combining for 61 points in those two areas compared to UConn's 34. "When you bring a group this talented together, and they decide from the beginning that they're going to do it this way and they never waver and they never change, that's probably the most uncommon thing in athletics now," Michigan coach Dusty May said. "For these guys to cut down the nets after all they've sacrificed is pretty special." UConn struggled to generate offense against Michigan's imposing frontcourt. The Huskies missed 13 straight three-point attempts at one stretch and shot 1-for-9 on shots contested by Aday Mara. Foul trouble compounded their difficulties, as Silas Demary Jr. fouled out and Solo Ball collected four fouls in the second half. Star forward Yaxel Lendeborg, playing through a sprained left MCL and ankle suffered against Arizona, managed just one first-half field goal. He rebounded with nine points and three rebounds after halftime. "I'm just so proud of myself, where I came from," Cadeau said. "Last year, I was really down on myself, a lot of people doubted me, and I'm just so proud of myself for me to be able to say I was the Most Outstanding Player and win a national championship at the same time." "They're legit," UConn coach Dan Hurley said. "They definitely deserved to win the national championship. They're clearly the best team in the country this year." Morez Johnson Jr. contributed 12 points and 10 rebounds for Michigan. The Wolverines' identity of size, length and interior presence proved decisive in their historic run.

Center Isaiah Stewart, out since March 15 with a left calf strain, was also elevated to doubtful before being ruled out Monday. "Both of them are progressing. They are doing more and more each day," said Pistons coach J.B. Bickerstaff. "With both of these things, it is always about how you respond the next day. I don't want to jump out there and say anything, but so far, so good. They are taking steps in the right direction." Bickerstaff expressed optimism that both Cunningham and Stewart will return before the postseason opens. The Detroit Pistons have three regular season games remaining. Detroit has proven resilient during Cunningham's absence, clinching the No. 1 overall seed in the Eastern Conference with a win over the Philadelphia 76ers on Saturday. The Pistons remain the most dominant team in the East despite losing their primary offensive engine. Before the injury, Cunningham was generating MVP consideration, averaging 24.5 points and 9.9 assists per game. Stewart's availability would also bolster a frontcourt depth the Pistons will need heading into a postseason run.

title: Victor Wembanyama Exits Monday's Game With Rib Injury, Award Eligibility At Risk
summary: The San Antonio Spurs ruled Victor Wembanyama out for the second half of Monday's game against the Philadelphia 76ers due to a left rib contusion. The 22-year-old big man sustained the injury in a collision with 76ers forward Paul George near half-court. The contact occurred with 10:47 remaining in the second quarter as Wembanyama received a pass during a transition sequence. He initially subbed out and went to the locker room before returning to the bench with 6:49 left in the first half. Wembanyama then requested a second substitution with 44.3 seconds before intermission and did not return after halftime. Wembanyama logged 15 minutes and 40 seconds, which will count toward postseason award eligibility. Players receive two exceptions to the standard 20-minute requirement, provided they play at least 15 minutes. He finished with 17 points, five rebounds and three blocks. The runaway favorite for NBA Defensive Player of the Year and a legitimate MVP contender , Wembanyama must play at least 20 minutes in one of San Antonio's final three games to preserve his award eligibility. The injury interrupts a dominant stretch for the French center. He had posted at least 30 points and 15 rebounds with three blocks in three consecutive games, tied for the second-longest such streak since blocks were officially tracked in 1973-74, trailing only Shaquille O'Neal's four-game run in 1999, per ESPN Research. Wembanyama entered Monday leading the San Antonio Spurs in scoring (24.9 points per game), rebounds (11.6) and blocks (3.1).
